findings.message: N+1 query pattern: get_item_names first SELECTs all Item.id values, then issues one session.get(Item, iid) per id to fetch each row's name. For N items this executes N+1 SQL round trips on an unbounded (table-sized) input — a clear performance regression that scales linearly with row count and will exhaust the connection pool / blow request latency SLOs under load.
findings.suggestion: Fetch only the name column in a single query: `return list(session.scalars(select(Item.name).order_by(Item.id)))`. This is one round trip, returns only the bytes needed, and removes the N+1 entirely. If full ORM objects are needed for other reasons, batch with `select(Item).where(Item.id.in_(item_ids))` instead of per-id session.get.

findings.message: Endpoint /items/names has no pagination or LIMIT and iterates the entire items table. As the table grows this becomes an unbounded scan + unbounded per-row round trip — accidentally O(N) work (and O(N) DB round trips with the N+1 above) on an input the client cannot constrain. A single request can monopolize a pool connection long enough to starve concurrent requests.
findings.suggestion: Add pagination (limit/offset or cursor) to the endpoint signature and apply `.limit(...)` in the query; document a maximum page size. This bounds both the query cost and the response size.

findings.message: Function body falls off the end without `return names`, so the endpoint will always return None and FastAPI will fail response_model validation (list[str]). While primarily a correctness issue, from a performance angle it means every call still performs all N+1 queries before failing — wasted DB work on every request.
findings.suggestion: Add `return names` (or, better, return the single-query result per the N+1 fix above).

findings.message: `from sqlalchemy import select` is performed inside the request handler. Import is cached after first call, but moving it to module top-level removes the per-call dict lookup and matches the style of the rest of the file.
findings.suggestion: Hoist `from sqlalchemy import select` to the module-level imports.
